text: The perforated appendix is observed to be walled off in the right pelvis, indicating localized inflammation and containment of the infection.
The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, and the surgeon begins to gently move the small bowel away from the site of the perforation.
The laparoscopic view shows adhesions and tissue densely adhered around the perforated appendix, indicating difficulty in separating the structures.
The surgeon identifies pus under pressure in the right pelvis during the laparoscopic appendectomy for perforated appendicitis.
The surgeon takes cultures from the area and irrigates the surgical field to clear away debris and fluid.
The surgeon carefully lifts a loop of ileum away from the area of perforation to locate the appendix.
The surgeon carefully irrigates the surgical field, ensuring thorough cleaning and removal of debris.
The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix and traces it down into the pelvis, visually locating the appendix within the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the lateral aspect of the terminal ileum mesentery, likely in preparation for further dissection or identification of anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs careful blunt dissection to free the appendix, then grasps it securely.
The surgeon visually identifies the tip of the appendix, which is exposed and visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates the ileal mesentery, visually tracing the anatomical structures to locate the appendix within the pelvic region.
The surgeon examines the surgical field from the opposite side, attempting to separate the terminal ileum mesentery from the sigmoid colon using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon reflects the ileum towards the upper abdomen, revealing the anatomical structures and facilitating further access to the appendix.
The surgeon observes the appendix positioned on the lateral aspect of the ileal mesentery, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully begins to dissect the appendix mesentery, ensuring not to encroach upon the adjacent ileum or ileal mesentery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to elevate and seal the body of the appendix, carefully manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully lifts the appendix mesentery away from the ileal mesentery, ensuring no inadvertent injury occurs during the separation.
The surgeon carefully dissects the appendix mesentery back towards the cecum, preparing the area for stapling.
The surgeon observes that the cecum and the base of the appendix appear normal, indicating a clear and unobstructed view for potential stapling or placement of a vessel loop.
The surgeon carefully dissects and releases the lateral attachments of the cecum to increase its mobility, facilitating further manipulation during the appendectomy.
The surgeon has freed the appendix from the pelvic sidewall, allowing for safer manipulation and dissection of the mesentery.
The surgeon continues to dissect the appendiceal mesentery, carefully separating it from the cecum to prepare for stapling.
The surgeon prepares to safely staple the base of the appendix, having completed the dissection of the appendiceal mesentery back to the cecum.
The surgeon observes the staple line, noting that it is hemostatic, indicating no visible bleeding along the stapled tissue.
The surgeon applies Surgicel to the ileal mesentery to achieve additional hemostasis.
The surgeon repositions the ileum and omentum over the surgical site, covering the area where the appendix was previously attached.
